title: Design a Notification System API
problemStatement: Design a RESTful API for a notification system that supports multiple channels (in-app, email, push). Users can view notifications, mark them as read, configure notification preferences per channel, and subscribe/unsubscribe from notification categories.
functionalRequirements: List notifications with filters (read/unread, category, channel)
Mark single or bulk notifications as read
Get and update notification preferences (per category per channel)
constraints: Notifications can be sent via multiple channels simultaneously
Users should control which channels they receive each category on
System notifications cannot be unsubscribed from

rubric.reasoning: Nine endpoints span three domains: notification CRUD, read state management, and preference/subscription management. Separating these concerns prevents a bloated notification endpoint that tries to do everything.

PUT for mark-as-read (updating existing state), DELETE for unsubscribe (removing a resource), POST for subscribe (creating a new resource). The key insight: marking as read is NOT creating something — it's updating state, so PUT is correct, not POST.
403 for attempting to unsubscribe from system notifications — the user IS authenticated (not 401) but the action is forbidden by business rules. 422 for invalid preference configurations.

The system notification constraint is the most interesting error case. Return 403 with a clear message: 'Security notifications cannot be disabled.' This is an authorization-level restriction, not a validation error.
Notifications are inherently user-specific. Every endpoint needs auth to know WHOSE notifications to show. No public notification endpoints make sense.

Notifications accumulate over time — pagination is essential. Consider cursor-based pagination since notifications arrive in real-time. Include unread count in the list response to avoid a separate /count call.

criteria: Filter by read status, category, channel
rubric.reasoning: Composable filters: ?read=false&category=orders shows unread order notifications. ?channel=email shows email notifications only. These filters help users triage their notification inbox.
Bulk mark-read accepts an array of IDs for efficiency. Alternative: mark ALL as read with no body. Preferences use a matrix structure: each category maps to enabled channels, giving granular control.

defendQuestions.question: Should new notifications be pushed to the client in real-time, or should the client poll the /notifications endpoint? What are the tradeoffs?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Polling every 5 seconds wastes bandwidth when there are no new notifications (99% of polls return nothing). Real-time push via WebSocket or Server-Sent Events (SSE) is more efficient — the server only sends data when notifications exist. SSE is simpler than WebSocket for one-directional push. Best approach: SSE for real-time delivery + REST API for historical listing and management. The REST API is the source of truth; SSE is the notification channel.

defendQuestions.question: Your preference model lets users control channels per category. What if a user wants to disable ALL notifications? Should you add a global mute, or rely on per-category settings?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: A global mute (PUT /preferences/mute with {muted: true}) is a UX essential — asking users to disable 20+ categories individually is painful. But it raises API design questions: does muting override system notifications? (Usually yes for push/email, no for in-app). Does unmuting restore previous preferences, or reset to defaults? Store mute as a separate flag that overrides per-category settings without modifying them, so unmuting restores the previous configuration.

defendQuestions.question: If a notification is sent to both email AND in-app, should it appear as one notification or two in the API?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: One logical notification with multiple delivery channels. The notification entity has an array of channels it was sent to. Marking the in-app notification as read should NOT mark the email as read (they're different delivery mechanisms). But GET /notifications should show one entry with channels: ['email', 'inApp'] rather than duplicate entries. This avoids confusing users with seemingly repeated notifications.

endpoints.description: List notifications with filters and pagination
Mark a single notification as read

designNotes: Separate 'mark as read' from notification CRUD — it's a state change, use PUT
Bulk operations accept an array of IDs for efficiency
Preferences are per-category per-channel — gives users granular control
System notifications (e.g., security alerts) should be non-unsubscribable — return 403
Include unread count in list response to avoid a separate API call
